content.problemStatement:
Given a RRR xCCC grid of lowercase characters and a string sss, check if sss is present in the grid. The string can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells, where **adjacent** cells are those horizontally or vertically neighbouring. The same letter cell may not be used more than once.


Input Format

The first line contains two integers RRR andandand CCC.
The next RRR lines contain CCC space-separated lowercase characters representing the grid.
The last line contains the string sss.


Output Format
Output true if the string s exists in the grid, otherwise output false.
constraints: All characters are lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers R and C. Next R lines each contain C space‑separated lowercase characters representing the grid. The last line of the test case contains the string s.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output "true" if the string s can be formed by moving horizontally or vertically to adjacent cells without reusing a cell, otherwise output "false". Each answer on its own line.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
3 4
a b c e
s f c s
a d e e
abcced
sampleTestCases.explanation: The word can be traced through adjacent cells as described in the statement.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
3 4
a b c e
s f c s
a d e e
see
sampleTestCases.explanation: The letters s→e→e are found by moving down twice and then right.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
3 4
a b c e
s f c s
a d e e
abcb
sampleTestCases.explanation: No path can form the word without reusing a cell.

solution.approach: We perform a depth‑first search from every cell that matches the first character of s. The recursive DFS tries to extend the current prefix by moving up, down, left or right to an unused neighbour that matches the next character. A visited matrix prevents reusing cells. When the index reaches the length of s we have found the word. Because the grid is at most 6×6, the exponential worst‑case (O(4^{|s|})) is easily fast enough.
solution.timeComplexity: O(R*C*4^{|s|}) per test case – acceptable for the given limits (R*C ≤ 36).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(R*C) for the visited matrix and recursion stack.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{static int R,C;static char[][] grid;static boolean[][] visited;static String word;static int len;static boolean dfs(int r,int c,int idx){if(idx==len)return true;if(r<0||r>=R||c<0||c>=C)return false;if(visited[r][c])return false;if(grid[r][c]!=word.charAt(idx))return false;visited[r][c]=true;boolean found=dfs(r-1,c,idx+1)||dfs(r+1,c,idx+1)||dfs(r,c-1,idx+1)||dfs(r,c+1,idx+1);visited[r][c]=false;return found;}public static void main(String[] args)throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringTokenizer st;int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();while(T-- >0){st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());R=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());C=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());grid=new char[R][C];for(int i=0;i<R;i++){st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());for(int j=0;j<C;j++){grid[i][j]=st.nextToken().charAt(0);}}word=br.readLine().trim();len=word.length();visited=new boolean[R][C];boolean ok=false;for(int i=0;i<R && !ok;i++){for(int j=0;j<C && !ok;j++){if(dfs(i,j,0))ok=true;}}sb.append(ok?"true":"false");if(T>0)sb.append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
